+The ChatApp API is divided into two distinct communication layers, each with a clearly scoped responsibility:
+
+**Connection Layer (`common.connector`)** â€” Operates at the application level, before users enter a chat room. It handles discovery of other users, negotiation for room creation/joining, and exchange of the remote stubs needed to communicate once inside a room. Entities at this layer interact with *IConnection* endpoints, not room-level receivers.
+
+**Communication Layer (`common.receiver`)** â€” Operates inside a chat room. Once membership is established, all in-room messages flow through *IMessageReceiver* endpoints. This layer is responsible for typed message dispatch (text, commands, unknown-type resolution) among room members.
+
+### Justification for Separation
+
+Separating these layers provides several architectural benefits:
+
+1. **Distinct lifecycles.** Connection negotiation is a one-time handshake; room communication is continuous and potentially long-lived. Mixing them would complicate state management and make it harder to reason about what is valid at any point in time.
+
+2. **Security and access control.** Not every remote endpoint needs to be reachable by every user at all times. A user's *IInitialConnection* can be publicly discoverable (e.g., registered in an RMI registry), while *IMessageReceiver* stubs are only shared with authenticated room members.
+
+3. **Independent extensibility.** New connection-level message types (e.g., new negotiation protocols) can be added without affecting the room communication layer, and vice versa. The two visitor hierarchies evolve independently.
+
+4. **Protocol clarity.** Having two separate `receiveMessage` method signatures â€” one typed to `IConnectionMsg` and one to `ICommunicationMsg` â€” makes it impossible to accidentally send a room-level message through a connection endpoint or vice versa, catching such errors at compile time.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Interface Specifications
+
+### 2.1 Connection Layer (`common.connector`)
+
+---
+
+#### `IConnection` (Remote)
+
+**Purpose:** The primary remote stub for connection-level communication. Represents a user's connection endpoint, accessible by other users over the network (RMI).
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+void receiveMessage(ConnectionDataPacket<? extends IConnectionMsg> packet)
+    throws RemoteException;
+```
+
+- Accepts any connection-layer data packet.
+- Dispatches the packet through the receiver's *ConnectionDataPacketAlgo* to the appropriate handler.
+- The packet's generic type bound `<? extends IConnectionMsg>` ensures only valid connection messages are accepted.
+
+**Role in design:** The central remote interface through which all connection-level messages flow. All negotiation (invitations, join requests, room sharing, quits) passes through this method.
+
+---
+
+#### `INamedConnection` (Serializable)
+
+**Purpose:** A serializable wrapper pairing an *IConnection* stub with a human-readable name. Passed around as a serializable object so peers can display user names in the UI and hold onto a reference to the connection.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+String getName();
+IConnection getConnection();
+```
+
+- `getName()` returns the display name of the user (e.g., "Alice").
+- `getConnection()` returns the underlying remote *IConnection* stub.
+
+**Role in design:** Enables the system to associate identity (a name) with a remote endpoint. Stored in room member lists and passed in connection messages like *IInviteMsg* and *ISendConnectionsMsg*.
+
+---
+
+#### `IInitialConnection` (Remote)
+
+**Purpose:** A publicly discoverable remote interface used for first contact between users who have not yet exchanged *IConnection* stubs. Typically registered in an RMI registry or equivalent lookup service.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+INamedConnection getNamedConnection() throws RemoteException;
+```
+
+- Returns the caller's *INamedConnection* so the contacting user can store a reference and begin sending connection messages.
+
+**Role in design:** Acts as the "front door" â€” the publicly known address used only once to bootstrap a connection. After this exchange, all further communication uses the *IConnection* obtained from *INamedConnection*.
+
+---
+
+#### `IRoom` (Serializable)
+
+**Purpose:** An immutable, serializable identifier for a chat room. Passed in invitation messages and room-listing responses so users can identify and reference specific rooms.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+String getName();
+UUID getUUID();
+```
+
+- `getName()` returns a human-readable room name.
+- `getUUID()` returns a universally unique identifier for the room, preventing collisions between rooms with the same display name.
+
+**Role in design:** Serves as the key/token that uniquely identifies a room during connection-layer negotiation. Not itself a remote object â€” it is a value type passed by copy in serialized messages.
+
+---
+
+#### `ConnectionDataPacket<T extends IConnectionMsg>` (Serializable)
+
+**Purpose:** A generic, type-narrowed data packet carrying a connection-layer message. Bundles the typed message payload with the sender's *INamedConnection* so the receiver always knows who sent it and can reply.
+
+**Fields / Methods (conceptual):**
+
+```java
+T getMessage();                        // the typed connection message
+INamedConnection getSender();          // who sent this packet
+Class<T> getMessageClass();            // runtime class for visitor dispatch
+```
+
+**Role in design:** The envelope that wraps every connection-level message. The `Class<T>` is the key used by *ConnectionDataPacketAlgo* to look up the right command (visitor case).
+
+---
+
+#### `ConnectionDataPacketAlgo` (Visitor)
+
+**Purpose:** A visitor/algorithm that processes *ConnectionDataPacket* objects by dispatching to type-keyed commands. Implements the Visitor pattern for the connection layer.
+
+**Conceptual structure:**
+
+```java
+class ConnectionDataPacketAlgo {
+    // Map<Class<?>, AConnectionDataPacketAlgoCmd<?>> cmdMap
+    void setCmd(Class<?> msgType, AConnectionDataPacketAlgoCmd<?> cmd);
+    void setDefaultCmd(AConnectionDataPacketAlgoCmd<?> cmd);
+    <T extends IConnectionMsg> void execute(
+        ConnectionDataPacket<T> packet, Object... params);
+}
+```
+
+- `execute` looks up the packet's message class in the command map.
+- If found, runs the registered command.
+- If not found, runs the default command (typically: request the command from the sender).
+
+**Role in design:** The central dispatch mechanism for the connection layer. Enables open/closed extensibility â€” new message types are handled by adding new commands, not modifying the algo.
+
+---
+
+### 2.2 Connection Message Types (`common.connector.messageType`)
+
+---
+
+#### `IConnectionMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Base marker interface for all connection-layer messages. Enables the generic type bound in *ConnectionDataPacket*.
+
+```java
+public interface IConnectionMsg {}
+```
+
+---
+
+#### `IInviteMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** An invitation from one user to another to join a specific chat room.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+IRoom getRoom();
+INamedMessageReceiver getSenderReceiver(); // sender's in-room receiver stub
+```
+
+- Carries the *IRoom* identifier for the room being created or joined.
+- Carries the inviting user's *INamedMessageReceiver* so the invited user can send messages back upon joining.
+
+**Flow:** Sent by the room host to a potential member via `IConnection.receiveMessage()`.
+
+---
+
+#### `IRequestJoinMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** A reply from an invited user accepting the invitation and requesting to be added to the room.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+IRoom getRoom();
+INamedMessageReceiver getRequesterReceiver(); // requester's in-room receiver
+```
+
+- Identifies which room the user wants to join.
+- Provides the requesting user's *INamedMessageReceiver* so the host can add them to the room's observer list.
+
+**Flow:** Sent back to the host's *IConnection* by the invited user.
+
+---
+
+#### `IQuitMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Notification that a user is disconnecting from a room or from the application entirely.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+IRoom getRoom(); // null if disconnecting from everything
+```
+
+- If `getRoom()` is non-null, the user is leaving a specific room.
+- If null, the user is fully disconnecting.
+
+**Flow:** Sent to each room host and connected peer; receivers remove the sender from their observer lists.
+
+---
+
+#### `IRequestRoomsMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Asks a connected user for their list of available rooms (rooms they host or are members of).
+
+**Methods:** (no additional data needed â€” the request itself is the signal)
+
+**Flow:** Sent by a user who wants to discover joinable rooms.
+
+---
+
+#### `ISendRoomsMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Response to *IRequestRoomsMsg* â€” a list of available rooms.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+List<IRoom> getRooms();
+```
+
+**Flow:** Sent by the contacted user back to the requester's *IConnection*.
+
+---
+
+#### `ISendConnectionsMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Shares the remote stubs of all current room members with a newly joined user, so they can communicate with everyone.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+List<INamedMessageReceiver> getReceivers();
+IRoom getRoom();
+```
+
+**Flow:** Sent by the room host to a new member after their join request is accepted.
+
+---
+
+#### `IConnectionExceptionMsg` and subtypes
+
+**Purpose:** A hierarchy of exception messages for connection-layer errors.
+
+Subtypes:
+- `IConnectionErrorMsg` â€” general unexpected error
+- `IConnectionFailureMsg` â€” connection to remote endpoint failed (network issue)
+- `IConnectionRejectionMsg` â€” deliberate refusal (e.g., room full, banned user)
+
+Each subtype carries relevant context (e.g., the failing *IRoom*, a reason string).
+
+---
+
+### 2.3 Communication Layer (`common.receiver`)
+
+---
+
+#### `IMessageReceiver` (Remote)
+
+**Purpose:** The in-room remote endpoint for a user. Once inside a room, all messages are sent via this interface. This is the room-level analog of *IConnection*.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+void receiveMessage(MessageDataPacket<? extends ICommunicationMsg> packet)
+    throws RemoteException;
+```
+
+- Accepts any room-level data packet.
+- Dispatches through the receiver's *MessageDataPacketAlgo*.
+
+**Role in design:** The core remote interface for in-room communication. All text messages, command requests, command responses, and other room-level messages flow through this method.
+
+---
+
+#### `INamedMessageReceiver` (Serializable)
+
+**Purpose:** Serializable wrapper pairing *IMessageReceiver* with a display name. Passed to room members so they can identify who is speaking and hold remote references.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+String getName();
+IMessageReceiver getReceiver();
+```
+
+**Role in design:** The in-room identity token. Stored in each participant's list of known room members. The sender field in *MessageDataPacket* is an *INamedMessageReceiver*.
+
+---
+
+#### `MessageDataPacket<T extends ICommunicationMsg>` (Serializable)
+
+**Purpose:** Generic, type-narrowed data packet for room-level messages. Bundles message payload, sender stub, and message class for visitor dispatch.
+
+**Fields / Methods (conceptual):**
+
+```java
+T getMessage();
+INamedMessageReceiver getSender();
+Class<T> getMessageClass();
+```
+
+**Role in design:** The envelope for all room-level communication. The `Class<T>` key enables the *MessageDataPacketAlgo* to dispatch to the correct command.
+
+---
+
+#### `MessageDataPacketAlgo` (Visitor)
+
+**Purpose:** Visitor/algorithm for processing *MessageDataPacket* objects. Dispatches to type-keyed *AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd* instances.
+
+**Conceptual structure:**
+
+```java
+class MessageDataPacketAlgo {
+    void setCmd(Class<?> msgType, AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd<?> cmd);
+    void setDefaultCmd(AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd<?> cmd);
+    <T extends ICommunicationMsg> void execute(
+        MessageDataPacket<T> packet, Object... params);
+}
+```
+
+- Default command: creates and sends an *IRequestCmdMsg* back to the sender asking for the processing command.
+- Known commands: execute registered handlers (e.g., display text for *ITextMsg*).
+
+**Role in design:** The extensibility engine for room communication. New message types are supported without touching existing code.
+
+---
+
+### 2.4 Communication Message Types (`common.receiver.messageType`)
+
+---
+
+#### `ICommunicationMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Base marker interface for all room-level messages.
+
+```java
+public interface ICommunicationMsg {}
+```
+
+---
+
+#### `ITextMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** A plain text message.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+String getText();
+```
+
+**Flow:** The sender wraps a string in an *ITextMsg*, puts it in a *MessageDataPacket*, and sends it to each room member's *IMessageReceiver*. The receiver's algo dispatches to the ITextMsg command, which calls `ICmd2ModelAdapter.displayText(text)`.
+
+---
+
+#### `IRequestCmdMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Sent by a receiver when it encounters an unknown message type. Requests the appropriate processing command from the original sender.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+Class<?> getUnknownMsgClass(); // the Class that was not recognized
+```
+
+**Flow:**
+1. Receiver's algo default case fires on unknown message type.
+2. Receiver sends *IRequestCmdMsg* to the sender's *IMessageReceiver*.
+3. Sender responds with *ISendCmdMsg*.
+
+---
+
+#### `ISendCmdMsg`
+
+**Purpose:** Response to *IRequestCmdMsg* â€” contains the *AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd* that knows how to process a given message type.
+
+**Methods:**
+
+```java
+Class<?> getTargetMsgClass();
+AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd<?> getCmd();
+```
+
+**Flow:**
+1. Sender receives *IRequestCmdMsg*.
+2. Sender wraps the appropriate command in *ISendCmdMsg* and sends it back.
+3. Receiver installs the command in its algo (`setCmd(targetClass, cmd)`).
+4. Receiver can now process future messages of that type â€” and may reprocess the original message.
+
+---
+
+#### `ICommunicationExceptionMsg` and subtypes
+
+**Purpose:** Exception message hierarchy for room-level errors.
+
+Subtypes:
+- `ICommunicationErrorMsg` â€” unexpected error during message processing
+- `ICommunicationFailureMsg` â€” delivery failure (peer unreachable)
+- `ICommunicationRejectionMsg` â€” message rejected by the receiver
+
+---
+
+### 2.5 Visitor Commands
+
+---
+
+#### `AConnectionDataPacketAlgoCmd<T extends IConnectionMsg>` (Abstract)
+
+**Purpose:** Abstract base class for commands that process *ConnectionDataPacket* objects. Follows the Command pattern within the Visitor.
+
+**Structure:**
+
+```java
+abstract class AConnectionDataPacketAlgoCmd<T extends IConnectionMsg> {
+    private ICmd2ModelAdapter model;
+
+    void setModel(ICmd2ModelAdapter model);
+    ICmd2ModelAdapter getModel();
+
+    abstract void execute(ConnectionDataPacket<T> packet, Object... params);
+}
+```
+
+- Concrete subclasses implement `execute` for a specific *IConnectionMsg* subtype.
+- The `model` reference provides access to local application services.
+
+---
+
+#### `AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd<T extends ICommunicationMsg>` (Abstract)
+
+**Purpose:** Abstract base class for commands that process *MessageDataPacket* objects. This is what gets sent via *ISendCmdMsg* to teach a receiver how to handle a new message type.
+
+**Structure:**
+
+```java
+abstract class AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd<T extends ICommunicationMsg> {
+    private ICmd2ModelAdapter model;
+
+    void setModel(ICmd2ModelAdapter model);
+    ICmd2ModelAdapter getModel();
+
+    abstract void execute(MessageDataPacket<T> packet, Object... params);
+}
+```
+
+- Because these commands can be sent over the network (via *ISendCmdMsg*), concrete implementations must be **Serializable**.
+- The `model` is injected after deserialization, since the local model reference cannot travel over the network.
+
+---
+
+#### `ICmd2ModelAdapter`
+
+**Purpose:** Adapter interface giving commands (visitor cases) access to the local application model's services. Decouples commands from specific model implementations.
+
+**Methods (representative):**
+
+```java
+void displayText(String text);
+void addComponent(JComponent component);
+JScrollPane getScrollPane();
+void showError(String message);
+// ... other model-specific services
+```
+
+- Each implementation provides model-specific services (displaying messages, adding UI components, accessing scroll panes, etc.).
+- Commands call these methods without knowing anything about the concrete model class.
+
+**Role in design:** The key to making *AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd* objects safely portable. The command carries logic; the model provides local resources. Injection of the model after deserialization keeps both concerns separate.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Message Flow
+
+### 3.1 Initial Connection Between Two Users
+
+**Scenario:** Alice wants to connect to Bob for the first time.
+
+1. **Discovery:** Alice obtains Bob's *IInitialConnection* stub from an RMI registry (or shared URL/lookup service).
+
+2. **Get named connection:** Alice calls `bob.getNamedConnection()` over RMI. Bob's server returns `INamedConnection` containing his display name ("Bob") and his *IConnection* stub.
+
+3. **Alice stores Bob's connection:** Alice saves Bob's *INamedConnection* in her contact list. She can now send connection-level messages to Bob.
+
+4. **Optional: Bob gets Alice's connection.** Bob may reciprocate by calling Alice's *IInitialConnection* or Alice may include her *INamedConnection* in the first message she sends, so Bob can reach her too.
+
+At this point, both users hold each other's *IConnection* stubs and can begin room negotiation.
+
+---
+
+### 3.2 Joining a Chat Room
+
+**Scenario:** Alice wants to invite Bob to join her room.
+
+1. **Alice creates a room.** Alice creates a new *IRoom* (name + UUID) and an *IMessageReceiver* for herself in that room.
+
+2. **Alice sends IInviteMsg.** Alice wraps `IInviteMsg` (containing her *IRoom* and her *INamedMessageReceiver*) in a *ConnectionDataPacket* with herself as sender, and calls `bob.getConnection().receiveMessage(packet)`.
+
+3. **Bob's algo dispatches.** Bob's *ConnectionDataPacketAlgo* finds the *IInviteMsg* command and executes it. Bob's UI presents the invitation.
+
+4. **Bob accepts: IRequestJoinMsg.** Bob creates his own *IMessageReceiver* for this room, wraps it in *IRequestJoinMsg*, and sends it to Alice via `alice.getConnection().receiveMessage(...)`.
+
+5. **Alice registers Bob.** Alice's *IRequestJoinMsg* handler adds Bob's *INamedMessageReceiver* to the room's observer list (Observer pattern: Bob is now subscribed).
+
+6. **Alice sends ISendConnectionsMsg.** Alice sends Bob a *ISendConnectionsMsg* containing the *INamedMessageReceiver* stubs of all other current room members. Bob stores these stubs.
+
+7. **Room is fully established.** Both Alice and Bob now hold each other's *IMessageReceiver* stubs and can exchange room messages.
+
+---
+
+### 3.3 Sending a Text Message in a Room
+
+**Scenario:** Alice sends "Hello!" to the room (which contains Alice, Bob, and Carol).
+
+1. **Alice creates the message.** Alice creates a concrete *ITextMsg* with `getText()` returning `"Hello!"`.
+
+2. **Alice wraps in a packet.** Alice creates `MessageDataPacket<ITextMsg>` with:
+   - Message: the *ITextMsg* instance
+   - Sender: Alice's *INamedMessageReceiver*
+   - Class: `ITextMsg.class`
+
+3. **Alice dispatches to all observers.** Alice iterates over the room's observer list (Bob and Carol) and calls `receiver.receiveMessage(packet)` on each (Observer pattern: notify all observers).
+
+4. **Each receiver's algo dispatches.** Bob's and Carol's *MessageDataPacketAlgo* instances look up `ITextMsg.class` in their command maps, find the registered *ITextMsg* command, and execute it.
+
+5. **Display.** Each *ITextMsg* command calls `model.displayText("Alice: Hello!")`, showing the message in the chat UI.
+
+---
+
+### 3.4 Handling an Unknown Message Type
+
+**Scenario:** Alice sends a custom *IGifMsg* to Bob, who has never seen this type.
+
+1. **Alice sends the packet.** Alice sends `MessageDataPacket<IGifMsg>` to Bob's *IMessageReceiver*.
+
+2. **Bob's algo misses.** Bob's *MessageDataPacketAlgo* looks up `IGifMsg.class` â€” not found. The default command fires.
+
+3. **Default command: IRequestCmdMsg.** Bob's default command creates an *IRequestCmdMsg* with `getUnknownMsgClass()` returning `IGifMsg.class`. It wraps this in a *MessageDataPacket* and sends it to the **original sender** (Alice) via `packet.getSender().getReceiver().receiveMessage(requestPacket)`.
+
+4. **Alice receives IRequestCmdMsg.** Alice's *MessageDataPacketAlgo* dispatches to the *IRequestCmdMsg* handler.
+
+5. **Alice sends ISendCmdMsg.** Alice's handler looks up the *AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd* for *IGifMsg* from her command registry. She wraps it in *ISendCmdMsg* (with `getTargetMsgClass()` = `IGifMsg.class`) and sends it to Bob.
+
+6. **Bob installs the command.** Bob's *ISendCmdMsg* handler calls `this.algo.setCmd(IGifMsg.class, receivedCmd)`. Bob then injects his local *ICmd2ModelAdapter* into the command (`cmd.setModel(model)`).
+
+7. **Bob re-processes.** Bob can now re-process the original *IGifMsg* packet (or simply wait for the next one). Future *IGifMsg* messages from any sender will be handled correctly.
+
+---
+
+### 3.5 Leaving a Room / Disconnecting
+
+**Scenario:** Bob wants to leave Alice's room.
+
+1. **Bob sends IQuitMsg.** Bob creates an *IQuitMsg* (with `getRoom()` returning Alice's *IRoom*) and sends it to every room member's *IConnection* (or the room host's *IConnection*, depending on architecture).
+
+2. **Alice and Carol process quit.** Their *IQuitMsg* command handlers remove Bob's *INamedMessageReceiver* from the room's observer list (Observer pattern: Bob is unregistered).
+
+3. **Optional: host notifies remaining members.** Alice, as host, may send an *ISendConnectionsMsg* to Carol with the updated member list (excluding Bob).
+
+4. **Full disconnect.** If Bob is disconnecting entirely (not just leaving a room), he sends *IQuitMsg* with a null room to all known *IConnection* peers, and all parties remove him from their contact lists.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Design Pattern Application
+
+### 4.1 Visitor Pattern â€” DataPacket Processing with Type-Keyed Dispatch
+
+The Visitor pattern is the backbone of message processing in both layers. The key insight is that messages are **typed data** and processing **algorithms** (visitors) are separate from the data.
+
+**Structure:**
+- *DataPacket* (and its specializations *ConnectionDataPacket*, *MessageDataPacket*) plays the role of the "element" â€” it carries a typed payload and knows its own message class.
+- *ConnectionDataPacketAlgo* / *MessageDataPacketAlgo* play the role of the "visitor" â€” they hold a map from message type to command (case).
+- *AConnectionDataPacketAlgoCmd* / *AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd* are the individual visitor "cases."
+
+**How dispatch works:**
+```
+packet.getMessageClass()  â†’  algo looks up class in cmdMap
+  Found?   â†’ execute registered command
+  Not found? â†’ execute default command (request cmd from sender)
+```
+
+**Key benefit:** New message types require only a new *AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd* subclass â€” no changes to the algo, no changes to existing commands. This is the Open/Closed Principle in action.
+
+**Unknown-type resolution** extends the Visitor pattern: instead of crashing or silently ignoring unknown types, the receiver actively *asks* the sender for the appropriate visitor case via *IRequestCmdMsg* / *ISendCmdMsg*. The command itself travels over the network (it is Serializable), and the local model is injected after arrival. This allows the system to be extended at runtime without recompilation.
+
+---
+
+### 4.2 Strategy Pattern â€” Room-Level Message Routing
+
+The Strategy pattern governs *how* messages are routed to room members. The routing policy is encapsulated as an interchangeable strategy object, allowing different rooms to use different dispatch behaviors.
+
+**Context:** The room host (or a routing manager) holds a reference to an *IRoutingStrategy* (or equivalent).
+
+**Concrete strategies:**
+
+- **BroadcastStrategy:** Iterates over all registered *IMessageReceiver* stubs and calls `receiveMessage()` on each. Used for general chat rooms where everyone sees everything.
+
+- **DirectMessageStrategy:** Looks up a specific recipient by name/UUID and sends only to that receiver. Used for private 1:1 messaging.
+
+- **CustomRoutingStrategy:** Game rooms, voting systems, or moderated rooms implement custom logic. For example, a voting room might count votes before forwarding; a game room might route moves only to the game engine.
+
+**Benefit:** Room behavior is fully encapsulated in the strategy. The sender always calls the same API (`sendToRoom(packet)`); the strategy decides delivery. New routing behaviors can be introduced without changing the sender, the receiver interfaces, or the algo.
+
+---
+
+### 4.3 Observer Pattern â€” Room Membership and Message Delivery
+
+The Observer pattern governs room membership. The chat room acts as the **subject** (observable), and room members are **observers** (subscribers).
+
+**Registration (joining):**
+When a user's *IRequestJoinMsg* is accepted, their *INamedMessageReceiver* is added to the room's observer list. They are now subscribed to all room events.
+
+**Notification (message delivery):**
+When a message is broadcast, the room iterates its observer list and calls `receiveMessage()` on each registered *IMessageReceiver*. This is the standard observer notification loop.
+
+**Deregistration (leaving):**
+When a user sends *IQuitMsg*, their *INamedMessageReceiver* is removed from the observer list. They no longer receive messages.
+
+**Benefit:** The sender does not need to know who is in the room. The room's observer list is the single source of truth for membership. Adding or removing observers does not require changes to the sending logic.
+
+---
+
+## 5. Extensibility
+
+### 5.1 New Message Types
+
+Adding a new message type (e.g., *IImageMsg*, *IFileTransferMsg*, *IPollMsg*) requires:
+
+1. Define a new interface extending `ICommunicationMsg` (or `IConnectionMsg` for connection-layer types).
+2. Create a concrete implementation class.
+3. Create a concrete `AMessageDataPacketAlgoCmd<IImageMsg>` that processes this type.
+4. Register the command in senders' *MessageDataPacketAlgo* instances.
+
+**Receivers who don't know the new type** automatically request the command via the *IRequestCmdMsg* / *ISendCmdMsg* protocol. Once installed, the command handles all future messages of that type. **No existing code is modified.** This is the Open/Closed Principle and the Law of Demeter in action.
+
+### 5.2 New Room Behaviors
+
+New room behaviors are added as new *IRoutingStrategy* implementations. A "Moderated Room" strategy, for instance, could queue messages awaiting moderator approval. A "Game Room" strategy could implement turn-based dispatch. The rest of the system â€” interfaces, message types, visitor infrastructure â€” is untouched.
+
+### 5.3 New UI Components
+
+New UI elements are exposed by extending *ICmd2ModelAdapter*. Commands access the model exclusively through this adapter. Adding a new model capability (e.g., `displayImagePanel(ImageIcon icon)`) only requires:
+1. Adding the method to the *ICmd2ModelAdapter* interface.
+2. Implementing it in the concrete adapter.
+3. Commands can now call the new method.
+
+Because commands go through the adapter rather than directly touching the model, the concrete model class can change freely, and multiple model implementations can coexist (useful for testing or for headless/non-GUI deployments).
+
+### 5.4 Multiple Implementations Interoperating
+
+The API's interfaces are defined in the shared `common` package. Two teams can independently implement the full system (different GUI toolkits, different internal data structures, different routing implementations) and still interoperate, because all network communication is through the shared Remote interfaces and Serializable message types. This is the fundamental promise of the design.
+
+---
+
+## 6. Error Handling
+
+### 6.1 Connection-Layer Errors
+
+**`IConnectionExceptionMsg` hierarchy:**
+
+- **`IConnectionErrorMsg`:** Wraps an unexpected exception that occurred during connection processing. Carries the `Throwable` cause and the offending packet. Sent back to the original requester's *IConnection*.
+
+- **`IConnectionFailureMsg`:** Indicates that a remote endpoint could not be reached (e.g., `RemoteException` when calling a stub). Triggered by the RMI runtime. Sent to the requester so they know the peer is unreachable and can remove it from contact lists.
+
+- **`IConnectionRejectionMsg`:** A deliberate refusal â€” room is full, user is banned, or the invitation was declined. Carries a human-readable reason. Sent in response to *IRequestJoinMsg* or *IInviteMsg*.
+
+**Handling `RemoteException`:** Every `receiveMessage` call is wrapped in a try/catch for `RemoteException`. On failure, the caller sends an *IConnectionFailureMsg* to any upstream requester and removes the unresponsive stub from its lists.
+
+---
+
+### 6.2 Communication-Layer Errors
+
+**`ICommunicationExceptionMsg` hierarchy:**
+
+- **`ICommunicationErrorMsg`:** Unexpected exception during room message processing. Carries the cause and original packet. Sent back to the sender so they know processing failed.
+
+- **`ICommunicationFailureMsg`:** A room member's *IMessageReceiver* is unreachable (network failure). The broadcaster catches the `RemoteException`, sends a failure notification to other members (or the host), and removes the unresponsive receiver from the observer list.
+
+- **`ICommunicationRejectionMsg`:** A receiver explicitly rejects a message (e.g., content policy violation, malformed payload). The receiver sends this back to the sender instead of processing the message.
+
+---
+
+### 6.3 Unknown Message Types (Graceful Degradation)
+
+Unknown message types are **not** treated as errors. Instead, the default visitor case proactively requests resolution from the sender via *IRequestCmdMsg* / *ISendCmdMsg*. This means:
+
+- The receiver **never silently discards** an unknown message â€” it tries to learn how to handle it.
+- If the sender cannot supply a command (e.g., it no longer holds the command), it sends an *ICommunicationErrorMsg* explaining this.
+- If the *ISendCmdMsg* is itself somehow malformed or the command fails to install, the receiver falls back to logging an error and notifying the sender.
+
+This design ensures the system degrades gracefully when encountering unfamiliar data, rather than throwing unchecked exceptions that could crash the receiver.
+
+---
+
+### 6.4 Guard against Stale Stubs
+
+When iterating the observer list to broadcast a message, each call to `receiveMessage()` is wrapped individually in a try/catch:
+
+```java
+for (INamedMessageReceiver nmr : observers) {
+    try {
+        nmr.getReceiver().receiveMessage(packet);
+    } catch (RemoteException e) {
+        // remove nmr from observer list, notify others
+        failedReceivers.add(nmr);
+    }
+}
+observers.removeAll(failedReceivers);
+```
+
+This prevents a single crashed client from disrupting message delivery to all other room members (fail-fast for one, continue for the rest).
